title: Refactor API layer to call real endpoints per openapi.yaml contract
description: All data fetching currently uses mock/stub data. Needs to be replaced with real HTTP calls to the API endpoints defined in openapi.yaml. Every domain (catalog, cart, checkout, orders, account) has a matching API surface — publishers, games, teams, characters, products, skus, cart, orders, payments. The refactor should wire each TanStack Query hook in src/modules/<domain>/hooks/ to the real endpoint via the shared HTTP client in src/api/, matching request/response shapes from the OpenAPI contract. Auth-gated endpoints must pass the bearer token. This is the highest-priority unblocked work before the app can function end-to-end.
close_reason: All API endpoints wired to real contract. Client param mapping fixed, server cart hooks added, orders/mine, payment-intent, skus all covered. MSW removed from dev. Tests green.

title: PRD: Admin Dashboard
description:
## Problem Statement

Internal staff have no UI to manage the merch shop. All catalog mutations (publishers, games, teams, characters, products, SKUs) and order inspection require direct API calls. This creates operational friction and a high error risk for non-technical staff.

## Solution

A protected /admin/* section within the existing app, accessible only to staff whose Clerk account carries publicMetadata.role === 'admin'. The dashboard provides CRUD for all catalog domains and a read-only orders list, using the admin API endpoints already specified in openapi.yaml.

## User Stories

1. As a staff member, I want the /admin/* routes to redirect me to /sign-in if I am not authenticated, so that the dashboard is never accessible to unauthenticated visitors.
2. As a staff member, I want the /admin/* routes to redirect me to / if I am signed in but not an admin, so that customer accounts cannot access staff tooling.
3. As a staff member, I want a sidebar navigation listing all admin sections (Orders, Publishers, Games, Teams, Characters, Products, SKUs), so that I can jump between areas without hunting for URLs.
4. As a staff member, I want to view a table of all orders with order ID, customer email, date, and total, so that I can monitor fulfillment and respond to support queries.
5. As a staff member, I want to click an order row to view its full detail (shipping address, line items, quantities, prices), so that I can answer customer questions about a specific order.
6. As a staff member, I want to create a publisher with a name and slug, so that new game publishers can be onboarded to the catalog.
7. As a staff member, I want to edit a publisher's name and slug, so that I can correct mistakes without deleting and recreating the record.
8. As a staff member, I want to delete a publisher, so that test or inactive publishers do not pollute the catalog.
9. As a staff member, I want to create a game under a publisher, so that new titles can be added to the storefront.
10. As a staff member, I want to edit a game's details, so that metadata stays accurate after launch.
11. As a staff member, I want to delete a game, so that discontinued or mislabeled titles can be removed.
12. As a staff member, I want to create, edit, and delete teams within a game, so that team-branded merchandise can be organized correctly.
13. As a staff member, I want to create, edit, and delete characters within a game, so that character-branded merchandise can be organized correctly.
14. As a staff member, I want to create a product and associate it with a publisher, game, team, or character, so that merchandise can be listed in the catalog.
15. As a staff member, I want to edit a product's name, description, and image, so that listing quality can be maintained over time.
16. As a staff member, I want to delete a product, so that discontinued items are removed from the storefront.
17. As a staff member, I want to create a SKU under a product with variant attributes and price, so that specific size/colour options are purchasable.
18. As a staff member, I want to toggle a SKU's availability, so that out-of-stock variants are suppressed without deleting the SKU.
19. As a staff member, I want to delete a SKU, so that discontinued variants are fully removed.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Module location**: new src/modules/admin/ domain, following the modular monolith convention established in ADR-0010. Exports through an index.ts barrel. No cross-module deep imports.
- **Route structure**: /admin nested under the TanStack Router tree. A single layout route at /admin uses beforeLoad (not a useEffect guard) to check authentication and role. All child routes (/admin/orders, /admin/publishers, etc.) inherit this guard automatically — no per-page guard duplication.
- **Auth check**: Clerk's useUser() exposes user.publicMetadata. The beforeLoad guard reads publicMetadata.role === 'admin'. If the user is not loaded yet, defer. If loaded and not signed in, redirect to /sign-in. If signed in but not admin, redirect to /.
- **Admin role provisioning**: set via Clerk dashboard (publicMetadata.role = 'admin' on the user object). No frontend provisioning UI in scope.
- **API client**: reuse the existing src/api/client.ts HTTP client. Admin endpoints are already in openapi.yaml — no new client infrastructure needed.
- **Orders (phase 1)**: read-only. Calls GET /orders (the admin list-all endpoint). No status update, cancel, or refund — those endpoints do not exist in the current openapi.yaml and are tracked separately (merch-shop-t5z).
- **Catalog CRUD**: each domain (publishers, games, teams, characters, products, SKUs) gets a list page and a create/edit/delete flow. Forms use Chakra UI v3 components per UI conventions.
- **Query hooks**: new hooks in src/modules/admin/hooks/ following TanStack Query conventions (typed key factories, hooks wrapped in custom hooks, components never import from @tanstack/react-query directly).

## Testing Decisions

Good tests assert external behavior visible to the user, not implementation details. They do not test which hook was called or which component was rendered — they test what appears on screen and what HTTP requests are made.

**The single seam**: renderRoute('/admin/...') from src/test-utils.tsx. This is the same seam used by account.orders.test.tsx and is the highest useful seam — it exercises the route guard, data fetch, and rendered output in one pass.

**Mock strategy**: vi.mock('@clerk/react') (same pattern as account.orders.test.tsx). mockUseAuth controls isLoaded/isSignedIn. mockUseUser controls user.publicMetadata.role. MSW server.use() overrides supply admin API fixtures.

**Required tests per section**:
- Auth guard: redirects unauthenticated user to /sign-in
- Auth guard: redirects signed-in non-admin to /
- Auth guard: renders admin content for user with role === 'admin'
- Orders list: renders order rows from MSW fixture
- Orders list: shows empty state when fixture returns []
- Each CRUD domain: list renders fixture rows; create/edit form submission fires correct MSW handler; delete confirm fires DELETE handler

**Prior art**: src/routes/account.orders.test.tsx — copy the mock setup, renderRoute call, and MSW override pattern directly.

## Out of Scope

- Publisher self-service admin (multi-tenant RBAC scoped per publisher)
- Order write operations (status update, cancel, refund) — tracked in merch-shop-t5z, blocked on backend
- Admin user provisioning UI (role assignment done via Clerk dashboard)
- Audit logging of admin actions
- Pagination or search on admin list views (add when list sizes warrant it)
- Analytics or reporting views

## Further Notes

- Infra issues merch-shop-u4r (route guard) and merch-shop-f0n (admin layout) must ship before any feature issues (merch-shop-w9q through merch-shop-ead) can begin.
- The orders write ops issue (merch-shop-t5z) is blocked on the backend team adding endpoints to openapi.yaml — do not start it until the spec is updated.
- All UI must use Chakra UI v3 components. Raw HTML elements with Chakra equivalents are banned. Run /chakra-ui-refactor before merging any admin UI.

title: Fix TypeScript errors in tests and admin view components
description:
## Why this exists

`npx tsc --noEmit` reports errors across 9 test files and 5 admin view components. These block CI type-checking and erode confidence in the type system.

## What needs to be done

### 1. Clerk mock partial stubs (9 test files + fixtures.ts)

`vi.mocked(useAuth).mockReturnValue({ isLoaded: true, isSignedIn: false })` fails because Clerk's `UseAuthReturn` requires ~10 more fields. Same for `UseUserReturn` and `UserResource`.

Fix: add typed auth-state constants to `src/mocks/fixtures.ts` using `as unknown as UseAuthReturn/UseUserReturn` (valid cast at test mock boundary per ADR-0010). Update all 9 test file call sites to use them instead of inline partial objects.

Also export `adminUser`, `buyerUser`, `fakerUser` typed `as unknown as UserResource` so typed user-context objects satisfy `UseUserReturn`.

Affected test files:
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.characters.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.games.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.teams.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.products.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.skus.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.orders.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(admin)/admin.publishers.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(account)/account.orders.test.tsx`
- `src/routes/(auth)/sign-in.test.tsx`

### 2. Order.status missing in fixture

`twoOrders` in `account.orders.test.tsx` is typed `Order[]` but missing the `status` field added to `src/api/types.ts`. Fix: add `status: 'pending'` to both order objects.

### 3. EmailAddressResource partial (sign-in.test.tsx)

`{ emailAddress: string }` passed where `EmailAddressResource` required. Fix: cast to `as unknown as EmailAddressResource`.

### 4. Box as="select" onChange type mismatch (5 admin components)

`onChange={(e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLSelectElement>) => ...}` is incompatible with Chakra Box's `ChangeEventHandler<HTMLDivElement>`. The explicit type annotation is wrong.

Fix: remove the explicit `e` type annotation; cast inside the body: `(e.target as HTMLSelectElement).value`.

Affected files:
- `src/modules/admin/components/AdminCharactersView.tsx` (L105)
- `src/modules/admin/components/AdminGamesView.tsx` (L105)
- `src/modules/admin/components/AdminTeamsView.tsx` (L105)
- `src/modules/admin/components/AdminProductsView.tsx` (L186, L202, L218, L234)
- `src/modules/admin/components/AdminSkusView.tsx` (L87)

### 5. handlers.ts duplicate id field (L387)

`id` specified twice in one object spread. Fix: remove the duplicate.

## Acceptance criteria

`npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0 with no errors. All existing Vitest tests continue to pass.
close_reason: All TS errors fixed: Clerk mock types, Order.status fixture, NativeSelectRoot/Field migration for Box-as-select, handlers.ts duplicate id. 171/171 tests pass, tsc --noEmit exits 0.

title: S9d: Cart merge on sign-in
description:
## Parent

merch-shop-1n3

## What to build

When a guest Buyer signs in via Clerk, any items in their sessionStorage cart are lost. Add a cart merge flow: on the `isSignedIn` false→true transition, read the current guest cart, POST it to the backend cart sync endpoint (mocked with MSW during development and tests), merge the response back into the local store, then clear sessionStorage.

Merge logic: if the same SKU appears in both guest and authenticated carts, sum the quantities. If only in guest cart, add it. If only in authenticated cart, keep it.

The backend sync endpoint is out of scope; implement against an MSW handler that accepts `POST /api/cart/sync` with a body of `{ items: CartItem[] }` and returns the merged cart.

Doing merch-shop-9sx (SSR guard) first is recommended — it makes the cart store safe in all render contexts — but is not a hard dependency.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] When a guest with items in cart signs in, those items appear in the cart after sign-in
- [ ] If the authenticated cart (returned by the sync endpoint) contains the same SKU, quantities are summed
- [ ] If the authenticated cart contains a different SKU, both SKUs appear in the merged cart
- [ ] sessionStorage cart is cleared after successful sync
- [ ] If the sync endpoint returns an error, guest cart items are preserved locally and an error is surfaced to the user
- [ ] Merge logic is unit-tested: two item arrays in, one merged array out, covering duplicate SKU and distinct SKU cases
- [ ] Integration test: pre-seed sessionStorage with cart items, mock Clerk `isSignedIn` flipping to true, mock `POST /api/cart/sync` returning a server cart, assert merged items visible in cart route
- [ ] Vitest passes

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately (merch-shop-9sx recommended first)

title: S9c: Mobile navigation drawer
description:
## Parent

merch-shop-1n3

## What to build

GlobalNav is a fixed horizontal flex layout with no responsive behaviour. On narrow viewports all nav links overflow or collapse invisibly. Add a hamburger button visible only on small screens; tapping it opens a Chakra Drawer containing the full nav: logo, cart link with badge, and auth state (sign-in/sign-up links or username + sign-out button). Desktop layout is unchanged.

Use Chakra's built-in responsive `hideFrom` / `hideBelow` props to toggle between desktop and mobile layouts — no new dependency needed. Chakra Drawer is already available.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] On viewports ≤ sm breakpoint, hamburger icon button is visible and desktop nav links are hidden
- [ ] On viewports > sm breakpoint, desktop nav links are visible and hamburger button is hidden
- [ ] Tapping the hamburger opens a Drawer containing: logo link, Cart link with item badge, auth links (sign-in / sign-up) or username + sign-out
- [ ] Cart badge count in the Drawer reflects the same store state as the desktop badge
- [ ] Auth state in the Drawer matches the desktop auth state (guest vs signed-in)
- [ ] Closing the Drawer (swipe, backdrop tap, or close button) works correctly
- [ ] Integration test: render root route with `window.innerWidth = 375`, assert hamburger visible and desktop links hidden, click hamburger, assert nav links visible in Drawer
- [ ] Vitest passes

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately

title: S9b: Route-level loading and error states
description:
## Parent

merch-shop-1n3

## What to build

Every data-fetching route currently shows a blank screen while TanStack Query is pending or when the API returns an error. The view components already have Skeleton/Spinner and error UI (visible in Storybook stories); the route layer just isn't passing `isPending` / `isError` down.

Wire `isPending` and `isError` (plus `refetch`) from each route's `useQuery` call into its view component. Add a single shared `QueryError` view component that renders a user-facing error message and a retry button; the retry button calls `refetch`.

Routes in scope: home/catalog, publisher page, game/catalog page, product detail page. Cart, checkout, and order history do not fetch on mount in the same way and are out of scope for this slice.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Catalog route shows Skeleton while products are loading
- [ ] Catalog route shows `QueryError` with a retry button when the API returns an error
- [ ] Publisher page shows Skeleton while publisher data is loading
- [ ] Publisher page shows `QueryError` with retry on API error
- [ ] Game page shows Skeleton while game/product data is loading
- [ ] Game page shows `QueryError` with retry on API error
- [ ] Product detail page shows Skeleton while product data is loading
- [ ] Product detail page shows `QueryError` with retry on API error
- [ ] Retry button calls the route's `refetch` and re-attempts the query
- [ ] `QueryError` is a single shared component reused by all routes
- [ ] Integration tests use MSW handlers that delay indefinitely (pending) and return 500 (error) to assert the above states
- [ ] Vitest passes

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately

title: S9a: Cart store SSR safety guard
description:
## Parent

merch-shop-1n3

## What to build

Guard the cart store's `sessionStorage` access so the module can be imported in a Node.js / SSR context without throwing. The store initialises by reading from `sessionStorage` at module load time; on the server that global does not exist and the app crashes before rendering.

Add a `typeof sessionStorage !== 'undefined'` check in the initialisation read and in the subscription write. When running server-side, the store starts with an empty cart and the subscription write is a no-op.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Importing the cart store module in a Node.js environment (no `window` / no `sessionStorage`) does not throw
- [ ] `cartStore.state.items` equals `[]` in that Node.js context
- [ ] Existing browser-side cart behaviour (read, write, subscribe) is unchanged
- [ ] Unit test covers the Node.js import path (mock `sessionStorage` as `undefined` before import, assert no exception and empty initial state)
- [ ] Vitest passes

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately

title: PRD: Remaining gaps — cart merge, mobile nav, loading/error states, SSR safety
description:
## Parent

merch-shop-1782195866539-1-b5cf2e6b

## Problem Statement

Four user-facing capabilities specified in the original PRD are not yet implemented. Buyers on mobile cannot navigate the shop. Buyers who sign in after shopping as guests lose their cart. Every data-fetching route shows a blank screen during load and on API failure. Cart state crashes the app when the page is server-rendered.

## Solution

Four thin vertical slices close the gaps end-to-end:

1. **Cart merge on sign-in** — when a guest signs in via Clerk, any items in sessionStorage are merged into the authenticated cart and synced to the backend. The guest sessionStorage is cleared after merge.
2. **Mobile navigation** — GlobalNav gains a hamburger button on narrow viewports. Tapping it opens a drawer (or collapsible panel) containing the same links as the desktop nav. Cart badge and auth state are visible in both layouts.
3. **Route-level loading & error states** — every data-fetching route passes `isLoading` and `isError` flags from TanStack Query down to its view component. View components already have Skeleton/Spinner and error UI; the wiring is missing.
4. **SSR safety** — the cart store's `load()` function guards against a missing `sessionStorage` (server/Node.js context) so TanStack Start SSR does not crash at module init.

## User Stories

1. As a Buyer who added items to my Cart as a guest, I want my Cart to persist when I sign in, so that I do not lose my intended purchase.
2. As a Buyer, I want items from my guest session merged with any existing items in my authenticated Cart, so that neither set of items is lost.
3. As a Buyer on mobile, I want to tap a hamburger icon to open the navigation menu, so that I can access all nav links without a full-width menu consuming screen space.
4. As a Buyer on mobile, I want the Cart badge and auth state (sign in / sign out) to be accessible from the mobile menu, so that I have full nav parity with desktop.
5. As a Buyer, I want to see a loading skeleton while product data is fetching, so that the UI feels responsive and I know content is coming.
6. As a Buyer, I want to see a clear error message when a data-fetching route fails, so that I am not left looking at a blank screen.
7. As a Buyer, I want the error state to offer a retry action, so that I can recover without a full page reload.
8. As a Buyer, I want fast page loads with SSR, so that the catalog is immediately visible without a loading spinner on first paint.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Cart merge trigger**: listen to Clerk's `useAuth` `isSignedIn` transition (false → true). On that event, read current sessionStorage cart, POST items to the backend cart sync endpoint, then clear sessionStorage. If the backend returns a merged cart, replace the local store with it.
- **Cart store SSR guard**: wrap the `sessionStorage` call in `load()` with `typeof sessionStorage !== 'undefined'`. Return `{ items: [] }` on the server path. The subscribe handler needs the same guard.
- **Mobile nav breakpoint**: use Chakra's responsive `display` props (`hideFrom` / `hideBelow`) to show the hamburger on `sm` and below, and hide desktop links. No new dependency needed — Chakra's `Drawer` component covers the slide-in panel.
- **Loading/error wiring**: each route's component reads `isPending` and `isError` from its `useQuery` call and passes them as props to the view component. View components already accept these props (Skeleton/Spinner states exist in Storybook stories). The fix is purely in the route layer.
- **Error UI pattern**: a shared `<QueryError onRetry={refetch} />` view component. One implementation, used by every route that can fail. Calls TanStack Query's `refetch` on retry click.
- **No new dependencies**: Chakra Drawer is already installed. TanStack Query's `isPending`/`isError` are already in scope. Clerk's `useAuth` is already imported in GlobalNav.

## Testing Decisions

A good test renders a full route via `renderRoute`, drives real user interactions, and asserts on visible output. It does not assert on store internals or query keys.

**Cart merge** — unit test in `src/store/cart.ts`: call merge function with two item arrays, assert deduplication and quantity addition. Integration test at `renderRoute('/cart')`: pre-seed `sessionStorage` with cart items, simulate Clerk `isSignedIn` flipping to true via MSW/mock, assert cart items still visible.

**Mobile nav** — integration test at `renderRoute('/')` with `window.innerWidth = 375` set before render. Assert hamburger button is visible. Click it. Assert nav links visible. Assert desktop links hidden (or not rendered).

**Loading/error states** — integration tests at each data-fetching route (`/`, `/:publisherSlug/:gameSlug`, `/:publisherSlug/:gameSlug/products/:productSlug`) using MSW handlers that delay indefinitely or return 500. Assert Skeleton visible during pending, error message visible on failure, retry button triggers refetch.

**SSR safety** — unit test: mock `sessionStorage` as `undefined` (delete from global), import cart store, assert `cartStore.state.items` equals `[]` and no exception thrown.

Prior art: `src/test-utils.tsx` `renderRoute()` is the shared seam for all route-level tests. Existing tests in `src/routes/cart.test.tsx`, `src/routes/checkout.test.tsx`, and `src/routes/account.orders.test.tsx` are the model to follow.

## Out of Scope

- Backend cart sync API implementation (separate project)
- Optimistic UI for cart merge conflicts
- Offline / service worker caching
- Push notifications for cart recovery
- Cart persistence in a database for guests (no account)
- i18n of loading/error copy
- Animated page transitions

## Further Notes

- The `noValidate` removal in `CheckoutFormView.tsx` is an unstaged change unrelated to these gaps — commit it separately before starting these slices.
- Cart merge depends on a backend endpoint (`POST /cart/sync` or equivalent). The slice should be implemented against an MSW mock of that endpoint; the real endpoint ships with the backend project.
- SSR safety (slice 4) is a prerequisite for enabling TanStack Start SSR in production; enabling SSR itself (configuring the server entry point) is out of scope for this PRD.

title: S9: Storybook — component library infrastructure
description:
## Problem Statement

Developers working on the esport merch shop have no isolated environment to build, document, or visually validate UI components. Every component change requires running the full TanStack Start app with MSW mocked data. There is no visual documentation for collaborators or designers, and no regression-testing surface to catch visual regressions introduced by Chakra UI theme changes or component refactors.

## Solution

Introduce Storybook v8 with the `@storybook/react-vite` framework, paired with a view/container component split. Each UI component is split into a pure view (in `src/components/ui/`) and a data-fetching container (in `src/components/`). View components have co-located stories covering all visual states. MSW handlers from the existing `src/mocks/handlers.ts` are reused in Storybook via `msw-storybook-addon`, enabling container-level stories that test the full fetch lifecycle without a running backend.

Development is done in parallel: for each component, the view is extracted and its stories written before moving to the next component. The split and stories ship together, not in two separate passes.

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to render `ProductCatalogView` in isolation so that I can work on card layout without running the full app.
2. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can validate the skeleton placeholder layout against the loaded state.
3. As a developer, I want an `Empty` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can verify the empty state copy and layout are intentional.
4. As a developer, I want a `Default` story for `ProductCatalogView` with four products so that I can validate the three-column grid at standard density.
5. As a developer, I want a `NoImages` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can verify the fallback placeholder renders correctly when `imageUrl` is absent.
6. As a developer, I want to render `PublisherNavView` in isolation so that I can develop navigation hierarchy without a router context.
7. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can validate the skeleton state for publisher links.
8. As a developer, I want a `Default` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can see all publishers and their game children with no active state.
9. As a developer, I want a `PublisherActive` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can verify the accent dot and active background render correctly for the active publisher.
10. As a developer, I want a `GameActive` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can verify both the active publisher and active game child are correctly highlighted.
11. As a developer, I want to render `PublisherPageView` in isolation so that I can validate the publisher header layout and accent top bar.
12. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `PublisherPageView` so that I can verify skeleton placeholders match the loaded header shape.
13. As a developer, I want a `Loaded` story for `PublisherPageView` with a real accent color so that I can verify the 2px accent bar and publisher avatar render correctly.
14. As a developer, I want to render `GamePageView` in isolation so that I can validate the game header and publisher subtitle.
15. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `GamePageView` so that I can verify the skeleton placeholder matches the loaded heading shape.
16. As a developer, I want a `Loaded` story for `GamePageView` so that I can verify the game name headline and publisher subtitle render together correctly.
17. As a developer, I want all stories to render in dark mode by default so that stories match the production app's locked dark theme.
18. As a developer, I want `renderLink` prop injection on all view components so that stories have no TanStack Router dependency and render without a router context.
19. As a developer, I want `msw-storybook-addon` configured globally so that MSW handlers from `src/mocks/handlers.ts` can be imported and reused in container-level stories.
20. As a developer, I want Storybook to use `@storybook/addon-interactions` so that interaction tests can be written as the component library grows.
21. As a designer, I want visual documentation of all component states so that I can review component behaviour without access to a running environment.
22. As a developer, I want the view/container split to follow a consistent file structure so that the pattern is predictable as new components are added.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Component split structure**: View (dumb) components live in `src/components/ui/`. Container (data-fetching) components remain in `src/components/`. Containers import their corresponding view from `ui/`.

- **Router decoupling via `renderLink` prop**: View components that render navigation links (`PublisherNavView`) accept a `renderLink: (href: string, children: ReactNode) => ReactNode` prop instead of importing `Link` from `@tanstack/react-router`. The container passes the real TanStack `Link`. Stories pass a plain `<a>` element. This breaks the router context dependency in all view stories.

- **Co-located stories**: Story files live next to their view component in `src/components/ui/`. `ProductCatalogView.tsx` and `ProductCatalogView.stories.tsx` are in the same directory.

- **MSW reuse**: The existing handlers in `src/mocks/handlers.ts` are imported directly into container-level stories via `msw-storybook-addon`. No new mock data is authored for Storybook — the single source of mock truth remains in `handlers.ts`.

- **Storybook configuration**: Storybook v8 with `@storybook/react-vite` framework (matches the Vite build). Global decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` wraps all stories in `ChakraProvider` with the custom `system` from `src/theme.ts` and sets `data-theme="dark"` to match production.

- **Parallel development workflow**: For each component, the view is extracted and stories are written before moving to the next component. The sequence per component is: extract view → update container to use view → write stories → move to next component.

- **Visual regression**: `@storybook/addon-interactions` is installed for interaction tests. Chromatic is explicitly deferred — no visual regression CI is wired at this stage.

- **Theme**: Dark mode only. No `@storybook/addon-themes` toolbar toggle. The app has no light mode; stories should not document a mode that does not exist.

- **View component states to cover**:

| View | Stories |
|---|---|
| `ProductCatalogView` | `Loading`, `Empty`, `Default`, `NoImages` |
| `PublisherNavView` | `Loading`, `Default`, `PublisherActive`, `GameActive` |
| `PublisherPageView` | `Loading`, `Loaded` |
| `GamePageView` | `Loading`, `Loaded` |

## Testing Decisions

A good story tests visual state and prop contracts, not internal implementation. Stories should be written against the view component's public prop interface only — no reaching into query hooks, no asserting on internal DOM structure.

- **`ProductCatalogView`**: Stories cover all four visual states by passing different `products` prop values (empty array, array without `imageUrl`, array with full data) and a `isLoading` flag. Prior art: the existing `isLoading` / `isError` branching in `ProductCatalog.tsx` defines the state space.

- **`PublisherNavView`**: Stories cover active state permutations by passing `activePublisherSlug` and `activeGameSlug` props with varying values. Prior art: `PublisherNav.tsx` active state logic.

- **`PublisherPageView` / `GamePageView`**: Stories exercise the accent color prop and loading skeleton. Prior art: the `--accent` CSS variable and skeleton branching in current page components.

- **No interaction tests at this stage**: `@storybook/addon-interactions` is installed but interaction stories (`play` functions) are out of scope for the initial Storybook setup. They are introduced per-component as behaviour grows.

## Out of Scope

- **Chromatic / Percy visual regression CI**: deferred until there is budget or the component library is large enough to justify automated baselines.
- **Light mode stories**: the app is dark-mode locked; no light mode toggle or `prefers-color-scheme` stories.
- **Container-level stories using `RouterProvider`**: view stories cover all visual states. Container stories with a real in-memory router are deferred until navigation behaviour needs regression coverage.
- **`@storybook/addon-a11y`**: accessibility auditing in Storybook is deferred; ARIA compliance is handled at the Chakra UI primitive level.
- **Storybook deployment**: no static Storybook deploy (GitHub Pages, Vercel) in this iteration.
- **New components**: this PRD covers only the four components that exist at time of writing. New components added in S3–S8 stories are out of scope here.

## Further Notes

The `renderLink` prop pattern established here becomes the project-wide convention for any view component that renders internal navigation. Document this in a short ADR once the pattern is proven across the four components.

The `msw-storybook-addon` version must be aligned with the MSW v2 API already used in `src/mocks/browser.ts` — use `msw-storybook-addon@2.x`, not `1.x`.

title: DS-4: Form control primitives
description:
## Parent

#11 — PRD: Design System — Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation

## What to build

A reusable `FormField` component that wraps Chakra's `Field`, `Label`, `HelperText`, and `ErrorMessage` into a single composable primitive with accessible validation states. This is the building block for all forms in the storefront — Clerk auth forms (S6) and the checkout address form (S7).

`FormField` accepts: `label`, `helperText` (optional), `error` (optional string — renders `ErrorMessage` and marks the field invalid when present), and `children` (the input element).

Deliver a demo form with at least one text input and one select that shows valid, helper-text, and error states — so the component is visually and behaviourally verifiable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `FormField` renders `Label` above its `children` input
- [ ] When `helperText` is provided, it is rendered and associated with the input via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] When `error` is provided, `ErrorMessage` is rendered, the input receives `aria-invalid="true"`, and `helperText` is hidden
- [ ] When `error` is absent, the field is in a valid state with no error markup in the DOM
- [ ] `FormField` is testable via `renderWithProviders` — assert on label text, error message text, and `aria-invalid` attribute
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

## Blocked by

#12 — DS-1: Theme extension config

title: DS-3: Drawer component pattern
description:

## What to build

This slice delivers a demo route or Storybook-style page that opens and closes the drawer so it is visually verifiable.

- [ ] Focus is trapped inside `AppDrawer` while open
- [ ] Focus returns to the trigger element when `AppDrawer` closes
- [ ] Clicking the overlay closes the drawer
- [ ] `AppDrawer` is testable via `renderWithProviders` — open state renders children, closed state does not
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

title: DS-2: Toaster / notification system

title: PRD: Design System — Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation
description:
## Problem Statement

Buyers interacting with the storefront across catalog browsing, cart management, checkout, and authentication expect accessible, consistent UI — keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, focus management, and screen reader support. Building these guarantees from scratch using Tailwind utility classes is error-prone and time-intensive. Tailwind ships no component primitives: every Modal, Select, Toast, and Drawer must be hand-rolled, with accessibility as the developer's sole responsibility. As the storefront grows from S2 through S8, the compounding cost of building inaccessible primitives and retrofitting them becomes a significant liability.

## Solution

Chakra UI v3 replaces Tailwind CSS as the design system. Chakra ships fully accessible, composable components built on Ark UI, with a token-based theming system. All new components are built using Chakra primitives. The app defaults to a dark theme matching the esports aesthetic. The default Chakra system token set is used now; brand tokens will be layered on top via `createSystem` when a design identity is established.

## User Stories

1. As a Buyer, I want interactive elements (modals, selects, drawers) to be keyboard-navigable, so that I can use the storefront without a mouse.
2. As a Buyer using a screen reader, I want all UI components to have correct ARIA roles and labels, so that I can navigate and use the storefront independently.
3. As a Buyer, I want focus to be managed correctly when modals and drawers open and close, so that I am never lost in the page.
4. As a Buyer, I want a consistent dark-themed UI across all pages, so that the storefront feels cohesive and on-brand for esports.
5. As a Buyer on mobile, I want touch-friendly tap targets and responsive layouts, so that the storefront is usable on a phone.
6. As a Buyer, I want loading states (skeletons) on product cards while data fetches, so that the layout does not shift or flash.
7. As a Buyer, I want error states to be clearly communicated with accessible color and text, so that I understand what went wrong.
8. As a Buyer, I want hover and focus states on interactive elements to be clearly visible, so that I always know what I am about to activate.
9. As a Buyer, I want form inputs (address, card number, quantity) to have visible labels and validation messages, so that I can complete checkout without confusion.
10. As a Buyer, I want toast notifications for cart actions (added, removed, updated) to be announced to screen readers, so that I know the action succeeded without a visual check.
11. As a Buyer, I want the Cart drawer to trap focus while open, so that I cannot accidentally interact with content behind it.
12. As a Buyer, I want the color contrast of all text to meet WCAG AA standards, so that I can read the storefront in varied lighting conditions.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Design system**: Chakra UI v3 (`@chakra-ui/react` 3.x). Tailwind CSS removed entirely — no coexistence, no utility class fallback.
- **Theme**: Default Chakra system (`defaultSystem`) used now. Custom brand tokens extend `createSystem` when design identity is established — no component rewrites required, only token overrides.
- **Color mode**: Dark default via `data-theme="dark"` on `<html>`. No user toggle for now.
- **Provider placement**: `ChakraProvider value={defaultSystem}` wraps children inside `RootDocument` in `__root.tsx`.
- **Component rule**: All new components use Chakra primitives (`Box`, `SimpleGrid`, `LinkBox`, `Skeleton`, `Card`, `Modal`, `Drawer`, etc.). Raw HTML elements with ad-hoc styling are a code-smell in this codebase.
- **Accessible card links**: Product cards use `LinkBox` + `LinkOverlay` for a single, accessible hit area per card.
- **Loading states**: `Skeleton` from Chakra replaces custom pulse animation divs.
- **ADR**: Decision recorded in `docs/adr/0007-chakra-ui-design-system.md`.

## Testing Decisions

- **What makes a good test**: Assert on externally visible behavior — rendered text, accessible roles, and user interactions. Never assert on Chakra prop values, class names, or component internals.
- **Seam**: `renderWithProviders` in `src/test-utils.tsx`. Wraps the component under test in `ChakraProvider` + `QueryClientProvider`. This is the single test seam for all component tests; no lower seam is needed.
- **Prior art**: `src/routes/index.test.tsx` — renders `ProductCatalog`, waits for MSW-intercepted data, asserts on product names in the DOM. All future component tests follow this pattern.
- **Accessibility assertions**: Use `@testing-library` queries by role (`getByRole('article')`, `getByRole('link', { name })`) rather than by class or test ID.

## Out of Scope

- Custom brand color palette or typography tokens (deferred until design identity is defined).
- Light mode or user-controlled color mode toggle.
- Visual regression testing (e.g. Chromatic, Percy).
- Chakra theming for third-party components (Clerk auth UI, Stripe Elements — these have their own theming APIs).

## Further Notes

The migration from Tailwind to Chakra was performed as a big-bang switch at S1 (one component existed at migration time), keeping the codebase free of coexistence debt. All stories from S2 onward build on Chakra exclusively. When brand tokens are introduced, they extend `createSystem` in a single config file without touching individual components.

title: S7: Checkout + Stripe payment + Order confirmation
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A checkout route (`/checkout`) where the Buyer enters a shipping address and pays. Stripe Elements handles the payment UI. The frontend calls the backend to create an Order and receive a Stripe PaymentIntent client secret, then confirms the payment client-side. On success, the Buyer is shown an Order confirmation page with an Order ID.

Guest Buyers complete checkout without an Account. The checkout form collects their email for the Order record.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/checkout` is reachable from the Cart view
- [ ] Shipping address form validates required fields before allowing payment
- [ ] Stripe Elements card field is rendered and accepts test card numbers
- [ ] Submitting with a valid test card transitions to the Order confirmation page
- [ ] Confirmation page shows Order ID and a summary of purchased SKUs
- [ ] Submitting with a declined test card shows an inline payment error without losing form state
- [ ] Route-level test: MSW mocks the Order creation endpoint; Stripe is mocked; test asserts confirmation page renders after successful submission

## Blocked by

- #8
close_reason: Checkout + Stripe + confirmation implemented with 5 Vitest behaviors, all passing

title: S5: Cart (add, update quantity, remove, subtotal)
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A Cart that accumulates SKUs across the session. The "Add to Cart" button on the product detail page adds the selected SKU. A cart view (sidebar or `/cart` route) shows all Cart items with quantity controls, remove buttons, and a running subtotal.

Guest Buyers carry their Cart in `sessionStorage`. Authenticated Buyers have their Cart synced to the backend on login (merge guest Cart into account Cart). Cart item count is shown in the nav.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Adding a SKU from the product detail page increments the Cart item count in the nav
- [ ] Cart view lists each SKU with name, variant, price, and quantity
- [ ] Incrementing or decrementing quantity updates the subtotal immediately
- [ ] Removing an item removes it from the Cart and updates the subtotal
- [ ] Guest Cart survives page refresh (persisted in `sessionStorage`)
- [ ] Signing in merges any guest Cart items into the Account Cart
- [ ] Route-level test: adds two SKUs, updates a quantity, removes one, asserts correct subtotal

## Blocked by

- #7
close_reason: Cart implemented: store, /cart route, GlobalNav badge, Add to Cart wired. 44/44 tests pass.

title: F1: Foundation — MSW + typed API client + test scaffold
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

Set up the shared infrastructure every other slice depends on:

1. **MSW** — install and configure Mock Service Worker. Service worker registered in `src/main.tsx` (dev only). A `src/mocks/` directory holds handler definitions, one file per resource (products, publishers, games, orders). Handlers mirror the future OpenAPI spec shape.
2. **Typed API client** — install `openapi-typescript` and `openapi-fetch`. Add a placeholder `openapi.yaml` spec at repo root with at least one endpoint stub so the generated types are non-empty. Wire up a typed `client` singleton at `src/api/client.ts`.
3. **Test render utility** — a helper at `src/test-utils.tsx` that wraps components with TanStack Router and TanStack Query providers, pre-seeded with MSW. All route-level tests import from here instead of `@testing-library/react` directly.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `pnpm dev` starts with MSW active and logs "MSW: enabled" in the browser console
- [ ] `pnpm test` passes with at least one smoke test that renders the root route using the test utility
- [ ] `src/api/client.ts` exports a typed client generated from `openapi.yaml`
- [ ] Adding a new MSW handler in `src/mocks/` is the only change needed to mock a new endpoint in both dev and tests

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

title: PRD: Esport Merch Shop — Initial Storefront
description:
## Problem Statement

Esport fans want to buy officially licensed merchandise for their favourite games, teams, and characters — but no single storefront spans multiple publishers and lets them filter by game, team, and character simultaneously. Buyers are forced to visit scattered publisher stores, each with different UX, checkout flows, and product ranges.

## Solution

A dark-themed, multi-publisher licensed merch storefront where Buyers browse Products by Game, Team, and Character facets, add SKUs to a Cart, and check out as a Guest or authenticated Account holder. Orders are fulfilled via drop-ship; no warehouse inventory is managed.

## User Stories

1. As a Buyer, I want to browse the full product catalog, so that I can discover available merch.
2. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Game, so that I only see merch for titles I care about.
3. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Team, so that I can find merch for my favourite esport organisation.
4. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Character, so that I can find merch featuring my favourite in-game figure.
5. As a Buyer, I want to combine Game, Team, and Character filters simultaneously, so that I can narrow results precisely (e.g. LoL + T1 + a specific character).
6. As a Buyer, I want to view a Product page with all available SKUs (size, colour, edition), so that I can choose the exact variant I want.
7. As a Buyer, I want to see the price and Availability of each SKU before adding it to my Cart, so that I do not attempt to purchase unavailable items.
8. As a Buyer, I want to add a SKU to my Cart, so that I can accumulate multiple items before paying.
9. As a Buyer, I want to view my Cart with a summary of SKUs, quantities, and subtotal, so that I can review my intended purchase.
10. As a Buyer, I want to update the quantity of a Cart item, so that I can buy more than one of the same SKU.
11. As a Buyer, I want to remove an item from my Cart, so that I can change my mind without abandoning the entire Cart.
12. As a Buyer, I want to proceed to checkout without creating an Account, so that I can complete my purchase quickly as a Guest.
13. As a Buyer, I want to optionally create an Account during or after checkout, so that I can access order history later.
14. As an Account holder, I want to sign in to my Account, so that my Cart persists across sessions.
15. As an Account holder, I want to view my Order history, so that I can track past purchases.
16. As a Buyer, I want to enter my shipping address at checkout, so that my Order is delivered to the right location.
17. As a Buyer, I want to pay via card, Apple Pay, or Google Pay using Stripe, so that I can use my preferred payment method.
18. As a Buyer, I want to receive an Order confirmation after payment, so that I know my purchase was successful.
19. As a Buyer, I want to browse Products by Publisher (e.g. Riot Games, Valve), so that I can explore merch within a specific brand.
20. As a Buyer, I want to see Publisher branding and Game artwork on product pages, so that the licensed nature of the merch is clear.
21. As a Buyer, I want to navigate the shop on mobile, so that I can browse and buy on my phone.
22. As a Buyer, I want fast page loads with SSR, so that the catalog is immediately visible without a loading spinner.
23. As a Buyer, I want to see a loading state when data is fetching, so that the UI feels responsive.
24. As a Buyer, I want to see a clear error state when the API is unavailable, so that I am not left looking at a blank page.
25. As a Buyer, I want SKUs that are unavailable to be visually indicated and non-purchasable, so that I do not select a variant I cannot buy.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Stack**: TanStack Start (React 19, TanStack Router, TanStack Query), Tailwind CSS v4, Clerk for auth, Stripe for payment. REST + OpenAPI contract to a separately hosted backend.
- **API layer**: All data fetching via `fetch` against an OpenAPI-specified REST API. No server functions that touch a database. A generated typed client (e.g. `openapi-typescript` + `openapi-fetch`) wraps all calls.
- **MSW for development**: MSW intercepts all API calls in the browser during development and in tests. Handlers mirror the OpenAPI spec. Removing MSW when the real backend ships requires no changes to component or hook code.
- **Domain hierarchy**: `Publisher → Game → Product`. URL structure reflects this: `/[publisher]/[game]/products/[slug]`.
- **Facets**: Products carry three orthogonal facets — Game, Team, Character. Filter state lives in the URL as query params so results are shareable.
- **SKU model**: Each purchasable variant (size + colour + edition combination) is a distinct SKU with its own price and Availability flag (boolean, not a stock count).
- **Cart**: Cart state is managed client-side (TanStack Store or React context). Authenticated Buyers sync their Cart to the backend on login. Guests carry Cart in `sessionStorage`.
- **Auth**: Clerk handles sign-up, sign-in, and session. Guest-to-Account upgrade is supported. Clerk JWTs are forwarded as Bearer tokens to the backend.
- **Checkout**: Stripe Elements for payment UI. On Order submission, the frontend calls the backend to create an Order and obtain a Stripe PaymentIntent client secret, then confirms the payment client-side.
- **UI theme**: Dark gaming aesthetic. Publisher and Game brand colours used as accent tokens. Tailwind CSS v4 custom theme variables per publisher.
- **Testing seam**: Route-level integration tests only. Render full TanStack Router route components with React Testing Library + MSW. MSW handlers are shared between dev mode and tests.

## Testing Decisions

A good test renders a full route, simulates Buyer interactions (clicks, form fills), and asserts on visible output (text, disabled states, navigation). It does not assert on component internals, query keys, or store shape.

Modules to test at the route level:
- Product catalog route (filter, list, pagination)
- Product detail route (SKU selection, add to Cart)
- Cart route (quantity update, remove, subtotal)
- Checkout route (address entry, payment, confirmation)
- Account sign-in / sign-up flows (Clerk UI integration)
- Order history route (authenticated only)

No prior art in the codebase — this is a greenfield repo. MSW setup and a test utility that wraps TanStack Router + Query providers will be the shared scaffolding for all route-level tests.

## Out of Scope

- Backend implementation (separate project)
- Inventory management, stock counts, or warehouse operations
- Admin / publisher portal for managing Products and SKUs
- Pre-orders, backorders, or waitlists
- Discount codes, promotions, or loyalty programmes
- Product reviews or ratings
- Internationalisation (i18n) and localisation beyond English
- Native mobile app

## Further Notes

- ADRs 0001–0006 in `docs/adr/` record the key architectural decisions made during design.
- `CONTEXT.md` at the repo root defines the canonical vocabulary — use it when naming routes, components, types, and API endpoints.
- `Character` is the canonical term for in-game figures across all publishers; never use Champion, Hero, or Agent in code or copy.

title: Playwright E2E infra: config, auth fixture, CI step
description:
## What to build

Set up standalone Playwright (not @vitest/browser-playwright) for full E2E runs. This is the foundation all other E2E slices depend on.

- playwright.config.ts at repo root: baseURL from env (PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL), chromium only, test dir e2e/, storageState for auth fixture
- globalSetup.ts: signs in with real Clerk test credentials (env: E2E_CLERK_EMAIL / E2E_CLERK_PASSWORD), saves storageState to e2e/.auth/user.json
- npm script test:e2e in package.json
- CI job step: install Playwright browsers, run test:e2e against dev server

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] npx playwright install chromium succeeds
- [ ] npm run test:e2e exits 0 against a running dev server
- [ ] globalSetup produces e2e/.auth/user.json with a valid Clerk session
- [ ] CI step runs headless and reports pass/fail
